concepts.title: Contents
concepts.body:
1. How scientists use AI to search for cures
2. AI minions/swarms and artificial trial level discovery
3. Genomics, CRISPR, RNA splicing and omics workflow
4. New chemistry, molecules, medicines and materials
5. A-Z science and mathematics field map
6. Major physics theories and law map
7. Expanded formula compendium by discipline
8. Practical guardrails and validation pipeline

concepts.section: 1. How Scientists Use AI to Search for Cures
concepts.title: 1. How Scientists Use AI to Search for Cures
concepts.body:
AI is used as a discovery accelerator across the disease pipeline: target discovery, mechanism modelling, structure prediction, molecular generation, virtual screening, toxicity prediction, biomarker discovery, trial matching, adaptive trial design, and post-market signal detection.
At the practical level, scientists create a closed loop: collect biological data -> train or fine-tune models -> generate hypotheses -> test in silico -> test in wet lab -> feed results back into the model -> repeat. The loop is valuable because biology has too many possible genes, proteins, pathways, molecules and patient subgroups for manual search alone.
Disease areas where AI is heavily applied include oncology, rare disease, immunology, neurology, infectious disease, cardiometabolic disease, autoimmune disease, gene therapy and antimicrobial resistance.
AI does not replace the scientist. It compresses search space. The validation still comes from reproducible experiments, clinical endpoints, safety evidence, manufacturing quality and regulatory review.

concepts.section: 2. AI Minions, Swarms and Artificial Trial-Level Discovery
concepts.title: 2. AI Minions, Swarms and Artificial Trial-Level Discovery
concepts.body:
An AI swarm is a multi-agent research architecture where different agents specialise: literature scout, genome analyst, protein modeller, chemistry generator, toxicity checker, trial simulator, regulatory checker, lab planner, patent mapper and cost optimiser.
A disease-cure swarm can run millions of cheap simulations before one expensive laboratory experiment. The swarm may propose targets, predict protein interactions, design guide RNAs, generate drug-like molecules, run ADMET filters, simulate patient cohorts, and rank only the strongest ideas for wet-lab testing.
Artificial trials include in-silico patient cohorts, digital twins, synthetic control arms, virtual dose-response modelling, organ-on-chip integration, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models and Bayesian adaptive trial simulations. These are not replacements for required human trials but can reduce waste and improve trial design.
Creating a new element is not a normal chemistry task. New elements require nuclear physics: heavy ion collisions, target nuclei, accelerator time, detection of decay chains and international validation. AI can optimise target/projectile choices and detector analysis, but it cannot create an element by ordinary molecular chemistry.

concepts.section: 3. Genomics, CRISPR, Splicing and Omics Workflow
concepts.title: 3. Genomics, CRISPR, Splicing and Omics Workflow
concepts.body:
Genomics AI identifies disease-linked variants, regulatory regions, copy number changes, structural variants, repeat expansions and polygenic risk patterns. It combines genome sequence, expression, methylation, chromatin accessibility, proteomics, metabolomics and clinical phenotype data.
CRISPR AI designs guide RNAs, predicts on-target activity, off-target risk, repair outcome, base-editing window, prime-editing efficiency, delivery constraints and immunogenicity risk. The target sequence, PAM, chromatin state, cell type and repair pathway all matter.
RNA splicing AI predicts splice donor/acceptor disruption, cryptic splice activation, exon skipping, intron retention and therapeutic rescue strategies such as antisense oligonucleotides or splice-switching molecules.
Single-cell AI maps cell states, rare subpopulations, immune exhaustion, tumour heterogeneity, developmental lineages and drug response differences at cell-by-cell resolution.

concepts.section: 4. New Chemistry, Medicines, Molecules and Materials
concepts.title: 4. New Chemistry, Medicines, Molecules and Materials
concepts.body:
AI drug discovery can generate novel molecules using graph neural networks, transformers, diffusion models, variational autoencoders, genetic algorithms and reinforcement learning. Molecules are filtered for potency, selectivity, solubility, permeability, metabolism, toxicity, synthetic accessibility, patent space and manufacturability.
In chemistry and materials science, AI searches catalysts, polymers, battery materials, perovskites, alloys, semiconductors, superconductors, membranes, enzymes and nanomaterials. The loop is design -> simulate -> synthesize -> test -> update model.
Naming a new medicine involves chemical names, internal development codes, international non-proprietary names, trademarks and regulatory review. AI can propose names, but official names follow controlled naming and safety rules.

concepts.section: 6. Major Physics Theories and Law Map
concepts.title: 6. Major Physics Theories and Law Map
concepts.body:
Classical mechanics: Newtonian laws, Lagrangian mechanics, Hamiltonian mechanics, rigid body dynamics, continuum mechanics.
Classical electromagnetism: Maxwell equations, Lorentz force, electromagnetic waves, optics, antennas, circuits.
Thermodynamics: Zeroth, first, second and third laws; engines; entropy; phase transitions.
Statistical mechanics: Macroscopic thermodynamics from microscopic probability and ensembles.
Special relativity: Constant speed of light, Lorentz symmetry, spacetime intervals, mass-energy equivalence.
General relativity: Gravity as spacetime curvature; Einstein field equation; black holes; cosmology.
Quantum mechanics: Wavefunctions, operators, uncertainty, measurement, spin, tunnelling.
Quantum field theory: Fields as quantum operators; particles as excitations; Standard Model basis.
Standard Model: Electromagnetic, weak and strong interactions with quarks, leptons, gauge bosons and Higgs.
Quantum electrodynamics: Quantum theory of light and charged particles.
Quantum chromodynamics: Strong interaction of quarks and gluons.
Electroweak theory: Unification of electromagnetic and weak interactions.
Condensed matter physics: Solids, semiconductors, superconductors, magnetism, topological phases.
Nuclear physics: Nuclei, decay, fission, fusion, binding energy.
Particle physics: Fundamental particles, accelerators, scattering, symmetries.
Plasma physics: Ionised gases, MHD, fusion, space plasmas.
Fluid dynamics: Navier-Stokes equations, turbulence, boundary layers.
Chaos and nonlinear dynamics: Sensitive dependence, attractors, bifurcations.
String theory / M-theory: Candidate quantum gravity framework using extended objects; not experimentally confirmed.
Loop quantum gravity: Candidate quantum geometry approach to gravity; not experimentally confirmed.
Cosmological inflation: Rapid early-universe expansion model.
Dark matter models: Matter component inferred from gravity; particle nature unresolved.
Dark energy / Lambda-CDM: Accelerated expansion model using cosmological constant or equivalent.

concepts.title: 7. Expanded Formula Compendium
concepts.body: Total formula/reference lines in this V2 compendium: 2401. Formula entries are grouped by discipline and written in plain technical notation so they survive Word/PDF rendering and can be searched, copied and expanded.

concepts.section: 8. Practical AI Discovery Pipeline and Guardrails
concepts.title: 8. Practical AI Discovery Pipeline and Guardrails
concepts.body:
Question definition: Define disease, endpoint, patient subgroup, mechanism and acceptable risk.
Data assembly: Collect genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, imaging, clinical records, literature and assay results.
Data quality: Remove leakage, batch effects, label errors, ancestry bias, duplicated samples and unvalidated endpoints.
Model build: Use appropriate models: GNNs for molecules, transformers for sequence/language, diffusion for generation, Bayesian models for uncertainty, survival models for outcomes.
Hypothesis generation: Generate target, guide RNA, molecule, antibody, protein binder, vaccine antigen, biomarker or trial design.
In-silico filtering: Apply potency, specificity, ADMET, PK/PD, manufacturability, off-target, immunogenicity and cost filters.
Wet-lab validation: Run biochemical, cell, organoid, animal or organ-on-chip assays where appropriate.
Clinical translation: Design phase I/II/III trials, inclusion/exclusion rules, biomarkers, endpoints, dosing and safety monitoring.
Regulatory and ethics: Document model, data provenance, bias, uncertainty, audit trail, human review and safety evidence.
Feedback loop: Feed failed and successful experiments back into active learning for the next cycle.

concepts.section: 9. AI Use by Niche Scientific Field
concepts.title: 9. AI Use by Niche Scientific Field
concepts.body:
Microbiology: AI designs antimicrobial peptides, predicts resistance genes, maps pathogen transmission, optimises phage therapy and searches microbiome-drug interactions.
Marine biology: AI monitors reefs, detects species from eDNA, predicts algal blooms, maps fisheries, studies marine microbiomes and searches marine natural products.
Oncology: AI finds driver mutations, predicts neoantigens, selects combination therapies, models tumour evolution and designs personalised trials.
Immunology: AI maps immune receptors, predicts antigen binding, designs antibodies, classifies immune cell states and monitors cytokine networks.
Neuroscience: AI analyses connectomes, neural signals, imaging, protein aggregation and patient stratification for neurodegenerative disease.
Materials science: AI searches batteries, catalysts, alloys, polymers, semiconductors, solar materials and high-entropy materials.
Astronomy: AI classifies galaxies, detects exoplanets, searches gravitational-wave signals and filters telescope surveys.
Climate science: AI downscales climate models, predicts extremes, assimilates satellite data and optimises energy systems.
Mathematics: AI assists conjecture search, symbolic regression, theorem proving, counterexample search and numerical experiment design.
Electrical engineering: AI optimises grid control, power electronics, battery management, fault detection, inverter performance and predictive maintenance.

concepts.title: 10. Glossary
concepts.body:
ADMET: Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion and toxicity.
Digital twin: Computational representation of a person, organ, disease process, device or system.
Active learning: Model chooses the next most informative experiment.
Foundation model: Large model pre-trained on broad data and adapted to many tasks.
Multi-agent system: Multiple specialist AI agents coordinating on a larger workflow.
Wet lab: Physical biological/chemical experiment.
Dry lab: Computational analysis or simulation.
PAM: Protospacer adjacent motif required by many CRISPR nucleases.
Off-target: Unintended edit or binding site.
Synthetic control arm: External or modelled comparator group used to support trial interpretation when appropriate.

swarm_roles.name: Literature Scout
swarm_roles.description: Scans expired-patent corpora, papers, and prior-art landscapes for relevant evidence and citations. Surfaces forgotten gems.

swarm_roles.name: Genome Analyst
swarm_roles.description: Maps variants, regulatory regions, copy-number changes, splice sites and polygenic risk. Reasons over sequence-level data.

swarm_roles.name: Protein Modeller
swarm_roles.description: Predicts structure, binding interfaces, allosteric sites, and ligand poses. Generates candidate folds and complexes.

swarm_roles.name: Chemistry Generator
swarm_roles.description: Proposes novel molecules / materials with target properties via graph neural networks, diffusion models, or genetic algorithms. Outputs candidates for downstream filtering.

swarm_roles.name: Toxicity Checker
swarm_roles.description: Filters candidate molecules and constructs for toxicity, off-target risk, mutagenicity and immunogenicity flags.

swarm_roles.name: Trial Simulator
swarm_roles.description: Runs in-silico trials, builds digital twins, simulates dose-response and PK/PD. Synthesises control arms; never replaces wet-lab or clinical evidence.

swarm_roles.name: Regulatory Reasoner
swarm_roles.description: Maps proposals onto regulatory frameworks, identifies submission gaps, drafts safety + ethics narratives. Reads regulations as first-class evidence.

swarm_roles.name: Experimental Designer
swarm_roles.description: Designs falsifiable experiments — endpoints, controls, sample size, blinding. Closes the loop between hypothesis and evidence.

swarm_roles.name: Formula Oracle
swarm_roles.description: Looks up and applies physical / mathematical / chemical formulas from the master reference; sanity-checks units and limits.

guardrails.detail: All initial candidates are evaluated only in simulation. No wet-lab artifacts are produced. Outputs are ranked hypotheses with confidence intervals, not actionable protocols.

Bench validation (organ-on-chip, in-vitro assays, biochemical characterisation) sits OUTSIDE the simulation. The system produces test plans, not lab procedures, and explicitly defers to qualified humans.
Preclinical work requires animal-welfare ethics review, approved protocols, and registered facilities. The simulation neither runs nor advises on live-animal procedures.
Human studies require IRB / ethics committee approval, informed consent, and regulator oversight. The simulation will refuse to produce dosing guidance for humans.
Regulatory submission requires manufacturing quality, GLP/GMP evidence, and complete safety dossiers. The simulation's Regulatory Reasoner role outlines gaps; humans complete them.

Absolute refusals: bioweapon design, weaponisable pathogens, gain-of-function for transmissibility, chemical-weapon synthesis, explosives, firearms conversion, nuclear weapons, live malware. These bypass all other reasoning.
